1: A revelation of God: the second assembly of the heavenly council with the Adversary echoes the first (Job 1:6) but with an intensified charge. The repetition of the cosmic-legal setting establishes that Job's suffering is not random or impersonal but part of an ongoing forensic process in the divine court. Christ's trial before Pilate has this same character: a human court reflecting a cosmic legal reality.
2: The theme: 'from roaming through the earth and walking back and forth on it' — repeated from Job 1:7. The Adversary's continued surveillance of the earth is the ongoing backdrop of human suffering. 1 Peter 5:8 'be sober-minded; be watchful. Your adversary the devil prowls around like a roaring lion, seeking someone to devour' — the danger is real and continuous; the defense is Christ's intercession (Hebrews 7:25).
3: A type: 'He still maintains his integrity, even though you incited me against him to ruin him without cause (ḥinnām).' The phrase 'without cause' is the Septuagint's ἐμάτην — and Psalms 35:19 / 69:4 use the same word for the enemies who hate the psalmist 'without cause.' John 15:25 applies Psalms 35:19 to Christ explicitly: 'they hated me without reason/without cause.' Job is ruined without cause; Christ is hated without cause — both the unjust sufferer whose suffering has redemptive power.
4: A revelation of God: 'Skin for skin! A man will give everything he has to save his own life.' The Adversary's cynical anthropology — that human piety is purely self-interested — is the doctrine of human selfishness the Incarnation and the cross directly refute. John 15:13 'greater love has no one than this: to lay down one's life for one's friends.' Mark 10:45 'the Son of Man did not come to be served, but to serve, and to give his life as a ransom for many.' Christ gives his life rather than preserving it — the complete inversion of the Adversary's claim.
5: A type: 'reach out now and strike his flesh and his bones' — the Adversary's request to strike the body of the innocent man is granted. This is Passion language: John 19:1 (the flogging); Job 19:17-18 (the crucifixion); Job 19:31-34 (the breaking of legs and the piercing). Christ's flesh and bones are struck so that his body becomes the site of redemption — the struck body of the scapegoat that carries away sin (Hebrews 13:12).
6: A direct contrast: 'Very well, he is in your hands. Only spare his life.' YHWH permits total bodily suffering but withholds Job's life. This is the inverse of the cross: for the Son, God does NOT spare — Romans 8:32 'he who did not spare (οὐκ ἐφείσατο) his own Son but gave him up for us all.' The contrast is deliberate: Job's life is spared so that the theological question can continue; Christ's life is not spared so that the redemptive answer can be given.
7: A type: 'The Adversary afflicted Job with painful, festering sores from the soles of his feet to the top of his head.' The top-to-bottom totality of the suffering echoes Isaiah 1:6 ('from the sole of the foot to the crown of the head there is no soundness, only wounds and welts and open sores'). Isaiah uses this as a description of Israel's covenant devastation; the same totality applies to the Passion's physical suffering (crown of thorns to nailed feet). Total suffering for the innocent one.
8: The theme: Job taking a piece of pottery to scrape his sores while sitting among the ashes — the image of the greatest man of the East reduced to ash-heap and broken pottery. Lamentations 3:16 'he has broken my teeth with gravel; he has trampled me in the dust.' Christ takes on this ultimate lowliness — Philippians 2:8 'he humbled himself by becoming obedient to death — even death on a cross'; the ash-heap as the furthest descent before the exaltation (Philippians 2:9).
9: A type: Job's wife says 'Are you still holding on to your integrity? Curse God and be done with it' — a temptation to abandon covenant faithfulness through death. The temptations of the cross mock Christ in the same way: Matthew 27:40 'come down from the cross, if you are the Son of God!'; Luke 23:37 'save yourself if you are the King of the Jews!' Both Job and Christ are urged to escape suffering by abandoning their covenant relationship with God; both refuse.
10: A type: 'Shall we accept good from God and refuse to accept adversity?' — the theological question that distills the book's challenge. Christ's Gethsemane prayer is the perfect expression of this acceptance: 'My Father, if it is possible, may this cup be taken from me. Yet not as I will, but as you will' (Matthew 26:39). The cup of adversity accepted rather than refused is the mechanism of redemption. 'Job did not sin with his lips' — the parallel with 1 Peter 2:22 ('no deceit was found in his mouth') is exact.
11: The theme: the three friends Eliphaz, Bildad, and Zophar come from distant regions to 'express their sympathy and comfort him' (lĕnûḏ lô wĕlĕnaḥămô). The Hebrew nāḥam (comfort) is the root of both the Comfort/consolation of Israel that Simeon waited for (Luke 2:25) and the Paraclete (ΠαράκλητοϚ = the one called alongside to help/comfort, John 14:16). The friends fail as comforters; Christ and his Spirit succeed.
12: A type: 'When they looked up from a distance, they could not even recognize him.' The radical disfigurement that makes Job unrecognizable is the same vocabulary as Isaiah 52:14: 'his appearance was so disfigured beyond that of any human being, and his form marred beyond human likeness.' The unrecognizable sufferer is the Servant whose disfigurement is simultaneous with his atoning work.
13: A revelation of God: 'They sat on the ground beside him for seven days and seven nights. No one spoke a single word to him, for they could see how overwhelming his suffering was.' The seven-day silent presence is the appropriate response to acute grief (the friends' later failure is speaking, not silence). Christ's presence with the grieving is similarly one of incarnate solidarity — John 11:35 'Jesus wept'; he sits with the grieving rather than immediately resolving the grief.
